summary: Pontiac City Council unanimously passed a six-month moratorium on the consideration of data centers 'pending further study and enactment of city regulation.' The resolution cited existing zoning insufficiency to prevent data centers' potential effects on public health, utilities, and infrastructure. City officials will review potential issues and propose a zoning ordinance during the moratorium period, which may be extended.
notes: Part of the growing wave of Michigan municipal moratoria (25+ communities). Not currently in database despite Sterling Heights, Howell Township, and Ypsilanti Township already being tracked.

summary: Hubbard Township trustees voted 2-1 to approve a resolution placing a one-year moratorium on data centers entering the township in February 2026. The township attorney reviewed the moratorium proposal and suggested it would be a wise idea, providing a 12-month window for the zoning commission to engage the public and develop appropriate regulations.
notes: Northeast Ohio municipality near Lordstown (which is already in database). Part of the ~18 Ohio municipalities with active moratoria.

summary: Vienna Township in northeast Ohio is using a pause on data centers to develop lasting zoning changes rather than a temporary moratorium. Trustee Phil Pegg requested zoning to place a limit of 85 decibels at the property line, and if a data center uses more than 5 megawatts of power, they must produce anything in excess on site or an adjacent site. All water usage and discharge must meet Ohio EPA regulations.
notes: Notable for taking a permanent zoning approach rather than a temporary moratorium. Northeast Ohio near Lordstown.

summary: Oregon City Council initially voted 3-2 with one abstention against granting a six-month extension to a developer for a proposed $2 billion data center near Corduroy and Wynn roads. However, the city law director later ruled that under Robert's Rules of Order, the abstention didn't count as a vote, meaning the ordinance actually passed 3-2. The situation remains contentious and legally uncertain.
notes: Northwest Ohio (Toledo area). Complicated procedural situation with the extension vote. Part of the broader NW Ohio pushback.

summary: Air Products is appealing to the Upper Macungie Township Zoning Hearing Board for a proposed 2.6 million-square-foot, three-building data center complex on their former 194-acre headquarters site along Cetronia Road. The project predates the township's new data center ordinance adopted December 4, 2025, so it would be reviewed under old zoning rules. Air Products requested a delay until May 27, 2026 to decide whether to proceed. Township adopted data center regulations in December 2025 but they won't apply to this project.
notes: Lehigh Valley, PA. Unique because the project predates the township's new data center ordinance. Community opposition is building in this region.

summary: Dickson City Borough Council adopted regulations that define, regulate and restrict data centers as special exceptions in the town's light manufacturing district, limiting data centers to Enterprise Street near Eagle Lane. The ordinance halted a proposed Bell Mountain data center campus. About 300 people attended the public hearing, with all 25 speakers either raising concerns about data centers or outright opposing them. Borough council stated 'We are not a sacrifice zone.'
notes: Part of the NEPA (Northeast Pennsylvania) data center cluster. Lackawanna County alone has at least 11 proposed data center campuses. Archbald and Blakely are already in database but Dickson City is separate.

summary: The Tarboro Town Council rejected a Special Use Permit for Energy Storage Solutions' proposed 300MW, eight-phase data center on a 52-acre site near Anaconda and McNair roads by a 7-1 vote. The developer filed an appeal to Edgecombe Superior Court in November 2025, alleging the town committed 'errors of law.' A separate project proposed for the Kingsboro Business Park continues. Edgecombe County commissioners also voted down another ESS project. Residents cited concerns about traffic, water use, 24/7 noise, and proximity to the animal shelter.
notes: Database has Edgecombe County but not Town of Tarboro specifically. The Tarboro SUP denial and subsequent court appeal is a distinct fight from the county-level action. ESS is appealing, making this ongoing.

summary: The New Hill Digital Campus proposed for an 89-acre site on Shearon Harris Road near the Harris Nuclear Plant faces massive community opposition. Nearly 5,000 people have signed a petition opposing the project. The facility would require ~250MW (25% of the nuclear plant's 1GW output) and up to 1 million gallons of reclaimed water per day. The town's wastewater facility is already nearing capacity. Apex Town Council spent 3+ hours examining the proposal; a decision is expected late summer/early fall 2026.
notes: Database has Wake County as an entry but Apex/New Hill is a distinct fight with its own massive petition (5,000 signatures). The Wake County entry may cover this but the Apex-specific fight is significant enough for its own entry.

summary: The largest permitted data center campus in the United States was approved in Pecos County in February 2026, developed by Pacifico Energy with approval from the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality. While the project has been approved, it represents a significant development in the Texas data center landscape. Local opposition details are limited compared to other TX fights, but the project is part of the broader Texas pushback environment.
notes: Approved but notable as the largest permitted campus in the US. Less clear opposition compared to other TX entries. Consider including if tracking all significant TX data center developments.

summary: Community members held a massive public meeting regarding the CoreSite DE3 data center being built in the Globeville/Elyria-Swansea neighborhood. CoreSite pulled out of the meeting at the last minute citing safety concerns, enraging hundreds of neighbors who showed up anyway. The overflow rooms were completely full with the outside crowd pounding on windows. The GES Coalition demands transparency around power/water use, environmental impact study, disclosure around diesel backup generator use, and high quality generators to limit emissions.
notes: Database has 'Denver (Elyria-Swansea)' which may cover this, but the Feb 25 meeting where the developer ditched and hundreds showed up is a significant escalation. The GES Coalition is a named opposition group. If the existing entry covers this, consider updating rather than adding.

summary: Linn County passed a new ordinance requiring data center builders to conduct environmental reviews, limit light pollution and noise, and compensate the county for infrastructure costs. This was triggered by Google informing the county in October 2025 of plans for a six-building campus in Palo alongside the Duane Arnold Energy Center (Iowa's sole nuclear plant). Residents at public hearings raised concerns about water use, electricity rates, light pollution, and low-frequency noise impacts on livestock. Some called for a complete moratorium.
notes: Database has 'Linn County (new ordinance)' which likely covers this. The Google hyperscaler connection and Duane Arnold nuclear plant proximity are important details to verify are captured.

summary: Residents of Mount Horeb have rallied opposition to proposed data center construction by local officials. The community is among several Wisconsin towns (alongside Beaver Dam, Port Washington, Janesville, and others) pushing back against data center development. Part of the broader Wisconsin opposition movement where 70% of registered voters say data center costs outweigh benefits.
notes: Mentioned in multiple articles as a Wisconsin community fighting data centers but limited specific details available. May need more research to flesh out.

summary: Senator Bernie Sanders called for a national moratorium on data center construction, aligning with Denver's moratorium announcement. Sanders argued that data centers are driving up electricity bills for American families while generating massive profits for tech companies. This was echoed by Ron DeSantis on the right, creating a bipartisan anti-data-center coalition. Over 230 state and local environmental groups sent a letter to Congress demanding a national moratorium in December 2025.
notes: Federal-level action. Database has CT federal entry but this Sanders/DeSantis bipartisan push is a distinct and major development. Should be tracked as it could reshape the entire landscape.

summary: Chatham County approved a 12-month moratorium on the development of new data centers and cryptocurrency mining facilities on February 13, 2026. The ban applies to all development approvals for data centers, data processing facilities, cryptocurrency mining operations and 'any uses associated with data processing facilities,' including web services, hosting, and genome sequencing. The moratorium applies to unincorporated areas and lasts through February 2027.
notes: Database already has Chatham County - verify this 2026 moratorium is captured. If so, this is a duplicate to skip.

summary: Gates County's Board of Commissioners unanimously approved a one-year moratorium on data centers in December 2025, the first NC county to do so. The ordinance prohibits accepting, processing, or approving applications for data centers, cryptocurrency mining, or other high-impact data processing facilities until December 17, 2026. Triggered by two inquiries from PACE, a firm that promotes data center development.
notes: Database already has Gates County - verify this entry is captured. If so, this is a duplicate to skip.

summary: Canton Town Council voted unanimously to pass a one-year moratorium on data center, cryptocurrency mining, and server farm construction after ~50 people voiced concerns at a specially called meeting. The hearing was prompted by inquiries from out-of-town companies about using the site of the town's decommissioned paper mill. Mayor Zeb Smathers called data centers 'a present threat to our community that can affect energy, water and lifestyle.'
notes: Database already has 'Town of Canton' - verify this entry is captured. If so, this is a duplicate to skip.

summary: Kings Mountain City Council voted 5-2 to approve a six-month moratorium on new data centers. The city already has one major data center on Shelby Road. Councilmember Annie Thombs stated 'Yes, I hear about the money it will bring to the city. That is great, but what else is it going to bring? We do not know. Everything that has a dollar sign in front of it does not mean it is good.'
notes: Database already has 'City of Kings Mountain' - verify this moratorium is captured. If so, this is a duplicate to skip.

summary: Marion County officials quietly approved a $2.4 billion data center (Project Liberty by Stream Data Centers) during a rare winter storm that paralyzed the state, using NDAs to keep the project hidden from residents. The council approved all three readings of tax breaks for Stream Data Centers in January 2026. Most residents were unaware the project was even on the table. Community backlash has been intense, with residents demanding transparency.
notes: Database has 'Marion County' and 'Marion County (Project Liberty)' entries - verify if the storm-timing secrecy angle and Stream Data Centers specifics are captured. If so, this is a duplicate.

summary: TigerDC formally withdrew its $3 billion Project Spero data center proposal from Spartanburg County after the county council voted not to offer a tax incentive following weeks of strong public opposition. Hundreds of residents showed up at a public hearing against the project, and thousands signed a petition. The 'No Data Centers in Spartanburg' Facebook page organized community opposition. County council stated it would not vote on any new data center projects until the state legislature establishes statewide rules.
notes: Database has 'Spartanburg County' - verify that the TigerDC withdrawal is captured. This is a major opposition victory with named opposition group. If existing entry covers this, update rather than add.

summary: Eagle Rock Partners applied to build an 859-acre, 1 GW, $6 billion data center on Cooks Hill Road near the ACE Basin - a 350,000-acre undeveloped estuary home to nine federally endangered species. Seven bipartisan state legislators officially opposed the project. Residents packed the Walterboro town auditorium in December to voice opposition. The Southern Environmental Law Center filed a lawsuit to block an ordinance making it easier for data centers in rural areas.
notes: Database has 'Colleton County' - verify if the ACE Basin specifics, Eagle Rock Partners, SELC lawsuit, and bipartisan legislative opposition are captured. If so, update rather than add.

summary: QTS is building a nine-building $1 billion campus near Lake Wylie, threatening the 95-acre Penland Christmas Tree Farm next door. Residents are concerned about noise, blinding security lights, and the sale of 400+ acres under the secretive code name 'Project COBRA' without community knowledge. Nearly 800 people signed a petition to stop the project. The tree farm owner worries increased heat could dry out his trees and ruin a nearly 60-year-old family business.
notes: Database has 'York County' SC entry - verify if Lake Wylie/QTS/Project COBRA specifics are captured including the Christmas tree farm angle. If so, update rather than add.

summary: Hood County commissioners rejected a proposed data center moratorium for the second time on February 24, 2026 by a 3-2 vote, after a state senator threatened legal action claiming counties lack authority to impose moratoriums. During an 8-hour meeting, the county attorney revealed a letter from Sen. Paul Bettencourt warning that counties have no constitutional or statutory authority to impose development moratoriums. Residents called for commissioner resignations. The county is instead seeking an opinion from the Texas Attorney General.
notes: Database has 'Hood County' TX - verify if the second moratorium rejection, AG opinion request, and senator's legal threat details are captured. If so, update rather than add.

summary: The Henrico Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously ruled on February 27, 2026 that Centra Logistics does not have a vested right to build a data center on Darbytown Road in Varina, upholding the Planning Director's December decision. The community celebrated: 'Finally somebody heard us.' Centra withdrew its PUP application in October after backlash and instead pursued a vested rights claim, which was now denied. Centra can appeal to Henrico Circuit Court.
notes: Database has 'Henrico County (Varina)' - verify the Feb 27 BZA denial is captured as an update. If so, update rather than add.

summary: Montour County commissioners unanimously denied Talen Energy's request to rezone 800+ acres from agricultural to industrial for a data center and power plant expansion near Montour Preserve. Commission chair stated 'No evidence was provided demonstrating that the benefits outweigh documented and foreseeable public harms.' The Concerned Citizens of Montour County grew from 4 members in August 2025 to hundreds, with ~3,000 petition signatures. Talen says it still plans to develop in the county.
notes: Database has 'Montour County' PA - verify that the Talen Energy rezoning denial, Concerned Citizens group name, and 3,000-signature petition are captured. If so, update rather than add.

summary: Lackawanna County Commissioner Bill Gaughan asked Governor Shapiro and the PA General Assembly for a three-year statewide moratorium on new large-scale data center approvals. The county alone has at least 11 proposed data center campuses, six in Archbald. State Senator Rosemary Brown announced support for a moratorium and unveiled a legislative package to restrict data centers to industrial zones. State Representatives Kyle Mullins and Kyle Donahue also back the legislation.
notes: Database has 'Lackawanna County' PA - verify if the Feb 2026 statewide moratorium push and legislative package are captured. If so, update rather than add.

summary: Residents continue to fight the $7 billion Stargate 1.4 GW data center despite MPSC approval of DTE's 19-year power supply agreement in December 2025. In February 2026, a judge denied resident Kathryn Haushalter's motion to intervene in the settlement between the developer and township. However, residents filed a separate mandamus complaint against Saline Township on January 28. Residents have called the project 'uniquely evil' citing environmental degradation, air and water pollution, and distrust of tech companies.
notes: Database has 'Saline Township' MI - verify if the 2026 legal developments (intervention denial, mandamus complaint) are captured. If so, update rather than add.

summary: South Carolina lawmakers are weighing the 'Data Center Development Act' to regulate data center expansion with guardrails on public resources and utility rates. The legislation would add a secondary state permitting layer. Industry leaders warn it could create 'friction' driving investment to other states. The debate was intensified by the secret Marion County approval and the Colleton County fight.
notes: No statewide SC legislative entry exists in the database. This is a significant new statewide legislative effort.

summary: Maryland lawmakers pushed forward a data center impact report, overriding Governor Moore's veto. The state is conducting a comprehensive study on data center impacts expected to conclude in September 2026. Multiple regulation bills are being considered in the 2026 session, including requirements for environmental review and ratepayer protections.
notes: Database has 'Maryland (statewide HB 120)' - verify if the veto override and 2026 regulatory push are captured. This may be a significant update to the existing entry.

summary: Oklahoma SB 1488 proposes a moratorium on building new data centers until November 1, 2029, while directing the Oklahoma Corporation Commission to study impacts on water supply, utility rates, property values, and siting. Sponsored by Republican state Sen. Kendal Sacchieri. A separate bill, HB 2992 (Data Center Consumer Ratepayer Protection Act), requires data centers to pay infrastructure costs and passed the House Utilities Policy Committee 7-0.
notes: Database has 'Oklahoma (statewide)' - verify if SB 1488 and HB 2992 specifics are captured. If so, update rather than add.

summary: The Charles County Planning Commission voted on March 2, 2026 to recommend denial of Zoning Text Amendment #25-187, which would have established zoning regulations for data centers in the county. Board members cited ongoing state impact study (due September 2026), concerns about electric demand and resident bills, and lack of clarity on self-generation and water usage. The recommendation moves to the Board of Commissioners for final consideration.
notes: Database has 'Charles County' MD - verify if the March 2 ZTA denial is captured. If so, update rather than add.

summary: Coweta's Planning Commission voted 4-1 to deny rezoning for Project Atlas, a controversial 270-acre data center proposed by Beale Infrastructure less than 30 miles southeast of Tulsa. Residents raised concerns about water supply and utilities going up. Beale offered $500K towards a water plant study and $3M for infrastructure upgrades. A subsequent city council vote on Feb 2 could overturn the planning commission denial.
notes: Database has 'Coweta' OK - verify if the Jan 19 planning commission denial and Beale Infrastructure details are captured. If so, update rather than add.

summary: Virginia is considering 60+ data center bills in the 2026 session. Key proposals: HB1515 would impose a temporary moratorium until July 2028 or until all existing interconnection requests are fulfilled. The Senate budget proposes phasing out the sales tax exemption (worth $1.6B/year) on Jan 1, 2027 - expedited from the original 2035 end date. A CCAN poll found 75% of voters blame data centers for rising utility costs and 9 in 10 believe lawmakers need to take action.
notes: Database likely has Virginia statewide entries but verify if the 2026 session's 60+ bills, HB1515 moratorium, and tax exemption phase-out proposals are captured. The Data Center Reform Coalition (41 orgs) is a significant named group.
